title: What Every Retail Stocktake Reveals About the Year's Actual Margin
excerpt: For bricks-and-mortar retailers, the June 30 stocktake is a compliance exercise. But it produces the one calculation that shows what every clearance and markdown actually cost across the year.
content:
For bricks-and-mortar retail businesses across Queensland and NSW, June 30 means a stocktake. Every item in the store is counted at cost, the closing stock figure goes to the accountant, and the annual accounts take their final shape. Most retail owners experience this as an obligation that marks the close of a trading year.

What the same exercise produces, taken one step further, is the year's actual gross margin: total sales revenue minus the cost of every item sold, including the full impact of every clearance line, end-of-season reduction, and promotional markdown made across the twelve months just closed. These pricing decisions are made individually through the year and feel correct in context. Their combined effect on annual gross margin is visible only when the full year of sales and cost data is assembled together.

The compliance work the stocktake supports is done exactly as it should be. The commercial analysis the same data enables is a different and worth-pursuing exercise, and for most retail business owners it is one that does not happen during the EOFY close.

What the Stocktake Data Actually Measures

The stocktake count, combined with the year's purchasing records, produces three commercially significant calculations.

The first is sell-through rate: the percentage of goods purchased during the year that were actually sold. For most retail categories, a sell-through rate of 75 to 85 percent across the full year reflects a buying plan reasonably calibrated to demand. Categories running consistently below 70 percent are carrying stock that ties up working capital without generating sales. The unsold inventory often requires a markdown decision rather than simply more time, and each markdown taken compounds the margin outcome for that category.

The second is shrinkage: the gap between goods received from suppliers and goods that can be accounted for as either sold or still in stock. Shrinkage covers theft, damage, administrative errors in receiving, and goods marked as received but not physically present. For most bricks-and-mortar retailers, shrinkage runs between one and two percent of revenue. Persistent shrinkage above that level is a direct deduction from gross margin with no offsetting revenue, and it is worth identifying before another trading year accumulates on top of it.

The third is actual gross margin percentage. Cost of goods sold, calculated from opening stock plus purchases minus closing stock, divided by total sales revenue, produces the real figure for the year. Comparing this to the theoretical margin implied by the business's standard pricing reveals the combined impact of all pricing decisions made during the year: the promotions, the clearance lines, the supplier cost increases absorbed rather than passed through, and any range or category mix shifts that moved revenue toward lower-margin products. A Cost and Margin Deep Dive from the EOFY data maps each category separately, ranking where margin was strongest and where the gap between intended and actual return was widest.

The Clearance Trade-Off That Is Often Underestimated

End-of-season clearance is a standard retail tool. Moving slow stock at reduced margin generates cash and frees shelf space for new season product. The commercial question is whether each clearance decision was favourable: did the cash recovered and the space freed for higher-margin product outweigh the margin surrendered on the discounted units?

Most retail owners make this assessment informally. A category-level review of clearance decisions across the year, using actual cost data and final sale prices, often surfaces a different picture. In some ranges, early clearance at a shallow discount recovered adequate margin and cleared space in time to benefit from new season sales. In others, clearance activity ran progressively deeper through the season and the final promotional layers sold units at or below cost once multiple reductions compounded on top of the original purchase price.

Understanding which categories responded well to clearance and which did not changes the buying plan for the next season. A business that overbought a slow-moving range in FY26 and needed four successive clearance rounds to shift it should be buying that range in lower quantities in FY27, or reconsidering whether it belongs in the mix at all. The EOFY data makes that assessment precise rather than approximate.

The Inventory Position That Shapes July and August

The closing stock figure at June 30 is capital committed to goods not yet sold. For a retail business with $180,000 in closing stock at cost and annual revenue of $1.2 million, that inventory represents roughly 55 days of cost-of-goods tied up in shelves and storerooms. That capital does not return to the business until each item is sold.

Businesses entering July with a lean inventory position have cash available and shelf capacity for new season product. Businesses entering with a heavy position from prior season stock must manage that existing inventory alongside new arrivals, frequently compressing the margin on both by running the old and new season in the same trading space and competing for the same customer's attention.

A Pricing Reset for the new season, built from the actual margin data the EOFY stocktake reveals, ensures the FY27 pricing structure reflects real cost rather than a general assumption that margins have held. For most retail businesses that work through this exercise, at least one category shows a margin position that has drifted below what the original pricing was designed to deliver, and the correction is more straightforward once the gap is precisely identified rather than felt.

For retail business owners thinking beyond the immediate trading year, the EOFY financial picture is also the foundation for any banking or valuation conversation. Lenders and buyers both look at inventory quality relative to cost, the sell-through history that indicates consistent demand management, and gross margin stability across multiple years. ProfitPulse works with retail business owners across Brisbane and the East Coast on the financial analysis that turns EOFY data into decisions for the year ahead. If the stocktake is happening this week and you want to use the data it produces commercially rather than only for the annual accounts, book a discovery call with ProfitPulse to talk through what the year's picture means for FY27.

faqs.q: What is a good gross margin percentage for a retail business in Australia?
faqs.a: Gross margin for bricks-and-mortar retail in Australia varies significantly by category. Fashion and apparel commonly achieve 45 to 60 percent. Homewares and gifts typically sit between 40 and 55 percent. Hardware and tools often run lower. The most useful benchmark is your own three-year trend rather than an industry average: whether gross margin is stable, improving, or drifting lower as clearance activity, supplier cost increases, and promotional discounting compound. A single year's figure tells less than the direction of travel across three years of trading data.

faqs.q: How do I calculate sell-through rate for my retail store?
faqs.a: Sell-through rate is calculated by dividing the number of units sold in a period by the total units available at the start of that period, then multiplying by 100. At EOFY, a full-year sell-through rate by category shows which merchandise moved consistently and which required repeated discounting to clear. Categories running below 70 percent for the year typically signal over-buying relative to actual demand, which compounds into markdown activity and working capital tied up in unsold stock at June 30.

faqs.q: Why does actual gross margin end up lower than expected in retail?
faqs.a: The gap between expected and actual gross margin in retail almost always traces to the cumulative effect of markdown decisions across the year. Each clearance event seems rational in context, but the combined impact of promotional discounts, end-of-season reductions, and supplier cost increases absorbed rather than passed through rarely gets measured in aggregate until EOFY. A Cost and Margin Deep Dive traces each category's actual margin outcome against original purchase cost to identify which decisions produced the most erosion and what the buying plan should reflect in response.

faqs.q: How does inventory shrinkage affect the profitability of a retail store?
faqs.a: Shrinkage is the difference between goods received from suppliers and goods that can be accounted for as either sold or still in stock. It covers theft, damage, receiving errors, and goods marked as received but not physically present. For most bricks-and-mortar retailers, shrinkage of one to two percent of revenue is within normal range. Persistent shrinkage above that level is a direct deduction from gross margin with no revenue offset, and each trading year it goes unaddressed compounds the total dollar impact on the business's profit.

faqs.q: What factors determine the valuation of a retail business in Queensland or Australia?
faqs.a: The primary drivers are normalised EBITDA, lease security and term remaining, inventory quality relative to its realisable value, gross margin consistency across multiple years, and owner dependence in buying and operational decisions. Retailers with consistent margins, documented buying processes, and teams that operate without the owner present in every decision attract meaningfully stronger multiples than owner-operated businesses where those characteristics are absent. An Exit Readiness Diagnostic identifies which factors are most compressing the current multiple and what structural changes would move it before any planned financing or sale event.

faqs.q: What financial metrics should a bricks-and-mortar retailer track every month?
faqs.a: Gross margin percentage by category, inventory turn rate, sell-through percentage by season, shrinkage rate, and debtor days for any wholesale or trade accounts are the metrics most directly connected to retail profitability. Gross margin percentage shows whether the cost structure and pricing are holding. Inventory turn measures how efficiently working capital is converting to sales. Sell-through tracks demand relative to buying quantities. Shrinkage captures loss outside the normal margin calculation. Together they give a clear picture of where the business is performing and where intervention is warranted each month.

faqs.q: How does a fractional CFO help a retail business owner in Brisbane or Queensland?
faqs.a: For retail owners managing buying, suppliers, floor operations, and financial administration simultaneously, a fractional CFO arrangement brings the monthly gross margin tracking, inventory analysis, and forward cash flow planning that most retail businesses intend to maintain but rarely sustain consistently. The practical value for retail is in tracking margin by category against targets each month rather than discovering at year end that clearance decisions have quietly eroded the gross margin the pricing was designed to deliver.
